skill.name: paper-2-web
skill.description: Convert academic papers into interactive websites, narrated videos, and print-ready conference posters using the Paper2All workflow.
skill.summary: Generate research websites, videos, and posters from LaTeX or PDF papers.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: Static analysis heavily over-counted markdown code fences and prose as Ruby execution and weak cryptography. The remaining confirmed risk is medium because the skill enables Bash workflows, third-party repository execution, system package installation, API key setup, and optional network-backed logo discovery. No evidence found of prompt injection, obfuscation, credential exfiltration, or malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Shell-Based Third-Party Pipeline Execution
medium_findings.description: The skill documents Bash commands for cloning Paper2All, installing dependencies, installing system packages, and running Python generation pipelines. These commands are relevant to the skill, but they can change the host environment and execute third-party code if an assistant runs them without user review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are clearly present in markdown examples and match the skill purpose. The risk is operational execution of third-party tooling, not confirmed malicious code.

medium_findings.title: API Credential Configuration and Optional Network Access
medium_findings.description: The documentation instructs users to create a .env file with OpenAI, OpenRouter, and Google API credentials. It also describes optional logo discovery through Google Search, so users should avoid exposing real keys in shared workspaces.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The files contain explicit .env and API key setup instructions plus optional network-backed logo discovery. No evidence found that the skill exfiltrates credentials.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fences Misclassified as Ruby Backticks
low_findings.description: Most external-command detections are markdown code blocks showing installation and usage examples. They are not Ruby backtick execution inside executable source files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed locations are fenced documentation examples, not Ruby source code. They still matter only if an assistant or user chooses to execute them.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Textual False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak-cryptography detections map to ordinary documentation words such as design, slides, codes, and description. No evidence found of MD5, SHA-1, DES, RC4, or cryptographic implementation usage.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Targeted review showed prose and output path examples, not cryptographic code. The static matches appear caused by substrings in normal documentation terms.

low_findings.title: Dangerous Combination Heuristic Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: The combined code execution, network, and credential signals come from setup documentation for a research generation tool. No evidence found of hidden exfiltration, obfuscation, prompt injection, or automatic credential transmission by the skill files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The risky ingredients are real but appear in transparent installation guidance. A search found no suspicious override language or malicious credential handling.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell-Based Third-Party Pipeline Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill documents Bash commands for cloning Paper2All, installing dependencies, installing system packages, and running Python generation pipelines. These commands are relevant to the skill, but they can change the host environment and execute third-party code if an assistant runs them without user review.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are clearly present in markdown examples and match the skill purpose. The risk is operational execution of third-party tooling, not confirmed malicious code.

dangerous_patterns.title: API Credential Configuration and Optional Network Access
dangerous_patterns.description: The documentation instructs users to create a .env file with OpenAI, OpenRouter, and Google API credentials. It also describes optional logo discovery through Google Search, so users should avoid exposing real keys in shared workspaces.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The files contain explicit .env and API key setup instructions plus optional network-backed logo discovery. No evidence found that the skill exfiltrates credentials.

content.user_title: Convert Papers Into Web, Video, and Posters
content.value_statement: Research papers are hard to share beyond the PDF. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code guide Paper2All workflows for websites, videos, and posters.
actual_capabilities: Guides setup for the Paper2All repository and required dependencies.
Explains how to generate interactive paper websites from LaTeX or PDF input.
Provides commands for narrated presentation videos from LaTeX papers.
Documents poster generation with custom conference dimensions and print output.
Describes batch workflows for multiple papers and shared output folders.
Lists review steps for website, poster, and video quality checks.
limitations: Requires external Paper2All code and dependencies outside the skill files.
API keys and paid model access may be required for full generation.
Talking-head video generation needs a high-memory NVIDIA GPU.
Generated scientific content still needs expert review for accuracy.

use_cases.title: Prepare a Conference Package
use_cases.description: Create a matching website, poster, and video for a submitted paper before a conference deadline.

use_cases.title: Publish a Preprint Homepage
use_cases.description: Turn a new preprint into an explorable page with figures, citations, and links for public sharing.

use_cases.title: Create Research Videos
use_cases.description: Generate narrated paper summaries for course materials, journal videos, or asynchronous seminar talks.

prompt_templates.title: Start With a Website
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me convert this academic paper folder into an interactive website. Check the input files first and explain each command before running it.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Poster
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a print-ready conference poster from my paper. Use 48 by 36 inches and include a review checklist for readability and figures.

prompt_templates.title: Generate a Video Abstract
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a five-minute video abstract from my LaTeX paper. Prioritize key findings, clear narration, and accurate figure use.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Full Dissemination Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a full Paper2All workflow for my paper collection. Include dependency checks, batch processing, cost controls, and final quality review steps.

output_examples.input: A user asks for a web page for a bioRxiv preprint.
output_examples.output: A workflow that checks paper assets, prepares the input folder, runs website generation, and lists review tasks before deployment.

output_examples.input: A user needs a poster for a virtual conference.
output_examples.output: A poster plan with size settings, required assets, QR code placement, and checks for text readability and link accuracy.

output_examples.input: A user has five papers from one lab.
output_examples.output: A batch processing strategy with folder naming, shared branding guidance, expected outputs, and manual review priorities.

best_practices: Ask before running setup commands, installing packages, or cloning third-party repositories.
Use clean LaTeX sources with all figures, tables, and bibliography files included.
Review generated outputs for factual accuracy, figure quality, links, and accessibility.
anti_patterns: Do not paste real API keys into prompts, logs, or shared examples.
Do not run full generation on untrusted papers without isolating the workspace.
Do not publish generated research summaries without author or subject expert review.

faq.question: What input formats does this skill support?
faq.answer: It documents workflows for LaTeX source folders and high-quality PDFs with selectable text.

faq.question: Can it create only one output type?
faq.answer: Yes. The references include separate workflows for websites, posters, and videos.

faq.question: Does it need API keys?
faq.answer: Most LLM-backed generation workflows need OpenAI or OpenRouter keys. Logo search may need Google credentials.

faq.question: Is the generated content publication ready?
faq.answer: No. Outputs should be reviewed for scientific accuracy, formatting, accessibility, and conference requirements.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill uses Bash workflows, third-party code, network setup, and API credentials, but no malicious behavior was found.

faq.question: Can it make talking-head videos?
faq.answer: The documentation describes optional talking-head generation, but it requires a suitable GPU and extra setup.
